coredata.dc:title: Coal-based carbon quantum dots modified Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF heterojunctions for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

coredata.dc:description: NS-CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using high-sulfur lignite as the carbon source. Modification of Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF using NS-CQDs to explore the performance of composite photocatalysts for hydrogen production. It was found that NS-CQDs significantly enhanced the hydrogen precipitation performance of the composite photocatalysts compared with the MOFs catalysts alone, and the highest hydrogen precipitation rate of the novel composite photocatalyst 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 reached 3662.68 μmol g−1 h−1. It can be inferred from the conduction band and valence band positions of Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF and NS-CQDs that a double S-type heterojunction is formed. The presence of thiophene and pyrrole nitrogen in NS-CQDs can promote electron transport, reduce the rate of electron-hole pair complexation, and improve the hydrogen evolution activity of the 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2.

head.ce:title: Coal-based carbon quantum dots modified Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF heterojunctions for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: NS-CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using high-sulfur lignite as the carbon source. Modification of Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF using NS-CQDs to explore the performance of composite photocatalysts for hydrogen production. It was found that NS-CQDs significantly enhanced the hydrogen precipitation performance of the composite photocatalysts compared with the MOFs catalysts alone, and the highest hydrogen precipitation rate of the novel composite photocatalyst 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 reached 3662.68 μmol g−1 h−1. It can be inferred from the conduction band and valence band positions of Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF and NS-CQDs that a double S-type heterojunction is formed. The presence of thiophene and pyrrole nitrogen in NS-CQDs can promote electron transport, reduce the rate of electron-hole pair complexation, and improve the hydrogen evolution activity of the 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2.

ce:section.ce:para: The current basic energy and environmental problems are becoming increasingly severe, and there is an urgent need to find clean energy to alleviate energy shortages and reduce environmental pollution caused by fuel combustion [1]. Hydrogen, as a typical representative of clean energy, has attracted many researchers to carry out continuous exploration. In particular, photocatalytic hydrogen precipitation is able to utilize solar energy to convert into hydrogen energy, realizing the efficient use of solar energy [2]. However, the photocatalysts that have emerged so far still have some drawbacks, such as slow photogenerated carrier separation efficiency, insufficient light absorption range, and fast electron-hole complexation efficiency [3]. To solve these similar problems, there is an urgent need to explore high performance photocatalysts.

Metal organic framework (MOF), with easy modification, tunability, diverse morphology, variety, and simple and easy preparation method [4]. A large number of applications have been carried out in photocatalysis, among which nanoflower-like Ni-MOF, which provides more available area for loading co-catalysts, can increase the surface active sites and enhance the reactivity [5]. There have been numerous research results on the photocatalysis of Zr-MOF, which has a three-dimensional structure with a regular arrangement of shapes, a large specific surface area, an ordered porous structure, a higher stability than that of room-temperature MOF, and a larger specific surface area than that of room-temperature MOF, which is more stable than that of room-temperature MOF. Zr-MOF is an excellent carrier because it is more excellent in stability than room temperature MOF materials. However, its photocatalytic hydrogen precipitation activity is low due to its wide band gap and easy electron-hole complexation [6], and efficient hydrogen precipitation cannot be realized without the participation of co-catalysts. Therefore, structural modification of Zr-MOF is needed to realize the efficient and stable use of Zr-MOF for photocatalytic hydrogen precipitation. Introduction of noble metals, which can effectively reduce the energy bands of the composites, provide more active sites, capture more available photogenerated carriers, promote electron-hole separation, and inhibit the rapid electron-hole recombination. Liu et al. [7] encapsulated C60 in a Zr-based NU-901 metal-organic skeleton (MOF) to obtain C60@NU-901 composites. C60@NU-901 photocatalytic H2 evolution rate reached 22.3 mmol g−1 h−1 under Pt modification. Zhang et al. [8] designed the Pt@UIO-66 composite. The hydrogen evolution efficiency of Pt@UiO-66 was 3871.4 μmol g−1 h−1, which was 50 times of that of single UiO-66. Liu [9] et al. designed a Pt@H-UiO-66-NH2/CdS composite catalyst with a photocatalytic hydrogen precipitation rate up to 12,810 μmol g−1 h−1. However, the precious metals are scarce in reserves and expensive, which makes them unsuitable for large-scale use. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple and inexpensive strategy to enhance the photocatalytic activity of Zr-MOFs.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are 0D carbon nanomaterials with a diameter of <10 nm [10]. CQDs have been used in many research applications in the fields of ion detection [11], electrocatalysis [12], and photocatalysis [13]. At present, CQDs are mostly synthesized by using small molecules as carbon sources, and some environmentally unfriendly reagents and medicines, such as strong acids and bases, are commonly used in the preparation process. However, the stability of CQDs prepared with small molecules as carbon source is poor, which is not suitable for large-scale preparation. Coal is a natural carbon source for the preparation of CQDs with abundant reserves, and coal-based CQDs have good stability [14]. The common methods for preparing CQDs include chemical oxidation, arc discharge and hydrothermal method. Hydrothermal method is a green and efficient method to prepare CQDs [15,16]. Yu et al. [17] used lignite as a carbon source to prepare CQDs, which were used to modify TiO2. The optimum composite catalyst 2 wt%CQDs/TiO2 showed the best catalytic activity for the photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B. Saikia et al. [18] used bituminous coal as a carbon source for the photocatalytic degradation of 2-nitrophenol by hydrothermal method to prepare CQDs. Jia et al. [19] used anthracite to prepare anthracite-based CQDs with controllable color using a two-step oxidation method. With the study of CQDs, it was found that elemental doping is the best way to improve the performance of CQDs, heteroatoms can introduce more active sites, by introducing heteroatoms can change the electronic distribution and surface structure of CQDs, and then come to enhance the catalytic activity. Elemental doping of N and S can increase the quantum yield by decreasing the energy gap between π-orbitals and the non-bonded (n) orbitals of carbon [20]. Nitrogen doping increases the structural defects of CQDs, which is favorable to provide more electrons and also reduces the band gap [21]. Awang et al. [22] prepared NCQDs using citric acid and urea as the carbon source, and composited them with nanosheets of g-C3N4 (CNS) to prepare a novel catalyst, CNQD-6, which degraded 62% of diclofenac sodium (DCF) in 3 h, confirming that NCQDs can effectively enhance the photocatalytic degradation activity. Sui et al. [23] used an electrochemical method to prepare CQDs, which were composited with titanium dioxide nanosheets (TiO2-001) to prepare a novel composite catalyst, CQDs/TiO2-001. CQDs/TiO2-001 was used for the photocatalytic decomposition of hydrogen precipitation from water, and was found to precipitate hydrogen at a rate of up to 7.9 μmol h−1, which is four times higher than that of single TiO2-001. Cheng et al. [24] prepared S,N-GOQDs using citric acid, sodium thiohydride, and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials, and the hydrogen precipitation efficiency could reach up to 71 mmol h−1 g−1 under optimal conditions, indicating that S,N-GOQDs have great advantages in photocatalytic decomposition of water. It can be seen that heteroatom doping can effectively promote charge separation and transfer, and the co-doping of S and N atoms can further promote charge separation and generate more available reactive electrons. Due to the small difference in electronegativity between C and S, it is difficult to incorporate S elements into coal-based CQDs by introducing sulfur sources from outside. However, high sulfur coal has a natural advantage in achieving in-situ doping of sulfur containing coal based CQDs.

In this study, nanoflower-like Ni-MOFs were prepared by solvothermal method, and an attempt was made to introduce Co ions in situ during the preparation of Zr-MOFs to enhance the catalytic activity of Zr-MOFs by introducing new metal sites. NS-CQDs were used to modify Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF to prepare composite catalysts, to investigate their photocatalytic decomposition of water hydrogen precipitation, and to analyze their photocatalytic hydrogen precipitation mechanism. This study focuses on improving the utilization value of lignite and provides a reference for coal-based CQDs in photocatalytic decomposition of water.

The coal samples were Lianhuatang lignite (LHT) from Yunnan, China. The experimental zirconium chloride (ZrCl4), urea (CO(NH2)2), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), terephthalic acid (C8H6O4), N,N-dimethylformamide (C3H7NO), eosin EY (C20H6Br4Na2O5)，Triethanolamine (C6H15NO3), anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH) were purchased from Aladdin.

Process coal blocks into coal powder and screen them to ensure particle size is less than 74 μm. The coal samples were subjected to industrial analysis, elemental analysis using Elementar Vario FL Cube elemental analyzer from Germany. Table 1 shows the parameters for coal sample analysis. LHT raw coal was used for the synthesis of S-CQDs.

Preparation of Co/Zr-MOF: 0.1 mmol of ZrCl4, 0.02 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.1 mmol of terephthalic acid (H2BDC), and 50 mL of DMF were put into a 100 mL glass beaker, and ultrasonication was carried out for 10 min to make the above medicines dispersed evenly in DMF. After ultrasonication, the beaker was stirred in a magnetic stirrer at room temperature for 20 min, and then the mixed liquid was transferred into a high-pressure reactor and held at 120 °C for 24 h. After the reaction was finished, the solid samples were collected by centrifugation. At the end of the first centrifugation, anhydrous ethanol was added and centrifuged again to wash away the impurities, and the process was repeated three times. Collect the sample after centrifugation, and the powder sample collected after drying is Co/Zr-MOF.

Preparation of Ni-MOF: 1.5 mmol of NiCl2·6H2O, 1.5 mmol of terephthalic acid, 64 mL of DMF, 4 mL of anhydrous ethanol, 4 mL of deionized H2O were put into a 100 mL glass beaker, and stirred at room temperature for 30 min in a magnetic stirrer, and then the above mixed liquids were transferred into a high-pressure reactor and held at 140 °C for 24 h. The sample was cooled to room temperature, centrifuged, and the solid sample was collected, then centrifuged several times, and anhydrous ethanol was added to wash away the impurities. Dry the washed sample, which is Ni-MOF.

CQDs were prepared by adding 2.00 g of coal powder and 60 mL of deionized water, add them to the reactor, and incubate at 180 °C for 10 h. It was cooled to room temperature, filtered by pumping, the liquid was collected, purified by dialysis using a 1000 Da dialysis bag, and freeze-dried at −40 °C to obtain S-doped CQDs powder, labeled as S-CQDs. Prepare NS-CQDs using the same method as CQDs, using 2.00 g of coal powder, 0.02 g of urea, and 60 mL of deionized water.

Preparation of composite catalyst: Weigh the Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF and CQDs proportionally, measure 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol, put them into a 50 mL glass beaker, and ultrasonic for 20 min to make the above medicines dispersed in the anhydrous ethanol uniformly. After ultrasonication, a water bath at 80 °C was heated and stirred until the anhydrous ethanol was completely evaporated, and the powder sample collected after drying was x%NS-CQDs/NCZy (x is the mass percentage of CQDs added, and y is the mass ratio of Ni-MOF to Co/Zr-MOF). Co/Zr-MOF, Ni-MOF, NS-CQDs, and 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 preparations process is shown in Fig. 1 .

Use X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku RINT-2000) to analyze the sample composition. Setting the current to 40 mA and the voltage to 40 kV. The sample morphology was analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI Talos F200S) with a test voltage of 200 KV. The catalysts were analyzed by the ASAP 2460 specific surface area and porosity analyzer (BET) from Micromeritics. The degassing temperature was 150 °C for 6 consecutive hours. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) of the Thermo Fisher ESCALAB Xi+ type characterized the elemental composition and binding energy changes of the catalysts. The test pressure was 6 × 10−7 Pa, the background pressure was 2 × 10−8 Pa. The peak positions were calibrated with C 1s (284.8 eV). Fluorescence testing was performed by a HoribaScientific fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4) with a xenon lamp as the light source.

The experiment was conducted in a 65 mL quartz bottle. The bottle was filled with 10 mg of catalyst, 20 mg of eosin, and 30 mL of TEOA solution. Ultrasound dispersion for 10 min, fill with N2 for 5 min to replace the air inside the bottle. The experiment was conducted in a 10 W LED (λ ≥ 420 nm) multi-channel photochemical reactor. The total duration of a reaction is 5 h. Every hour, take 0.5 mL of gas for detection. Hydrogen gas was detected by gas chromatography (Tianmei GC7900, TCD, 13Xcolumn, N2 as carrier gas).

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns and CQDs Raman spectra of each sample. From Fig. 2 (a), the characteristic peaks of Ni-MOF and Zr-MOF are consistent with those in the literature. For Ni-MOF, the characteristic peaks at 8.7°, 14.7°, 15.7° and 16.9° correspond to (200), (001), (201) and (−201) crystal faces, respectively [25]. The characteristic XRD peaks at 2θ = 7.4°, 8.5°and 25.7° can be attributed to the (111), (002) and (224) planes of Zr-MOF [26]. This indicates that Ni-MOF and Zr-MOF have been successfully prepared. The Zr-MOF doped by Co ions has the same major characteristic peaks as the single Zr-MOF, and no obvious new peaks appear, indicating that the Co ions are less doped and highly dispersed, and a small amount of Co ions can be doped into the lattice of Zr-MOF [27]. In Fig. 2(b), it can be found that Co/Zr-MOF shows a decrease in the 2θ value corresponding to the characteristic peaks compared to Zr-MOF, which is caused by the lattice expansion due to the incorporation of Co ions into the lattice of Zr-MOF. The lattice expansion is favorable to improve the photocatalytic activity [28]. The 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 also showed a certain degree of shift in the corresponding correlation characteristic peaks compared to Co/Zr-MOF and Ni-MOF, which indicated the existence of strong interfacial interactions between NS-CQDs, Co/Zr-MOF and Ni-MOF, suggesting that the 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 had been successfully prepared. In Fig. 2(a) and (b) NCZ2 has the characteristic peaks of both Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF, indicating that NCZ2 has been successfully prepared. By comparing the spectra of NCZ2 with 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2, it was found that there was no significant difference between the two, partly because of the small amount of NS-CQDs added, and partly because of the highly dispersed NS-CQDs.

Fig. 2(c) shows the XRD profiles of the samples with different ratios, each of which has both the XRD peaks of Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF, and the corresponding peak intensities are gradually increased with the increase of the proportion of Ni-MOF, which indicates that the composite catalysts have been successfully prepared. Fig. 2(d) shows the Raman characterization results of S-CQDs and NS-CQDs, with D peaks near 1350 cm−1 and G peaks near 1580 cm−1 [29]. The ID/IG of S-CQDs is 1.02, and that of NS-CQDs is 1.06, and the CQDs have been doped by the elements, which makes the value of ID/IG larger. The reason is that the doping of N element introduces more defects on the surface and edges of CQDs [30], which enhances the disorder of CQDs, which will be beneficial for improving the photocatalytic activity. Fig. 2(e) shows the Raman spectra of Ni-MOF and 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2. The structures of Ni-MOF and 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 were further analyzed using Raman spectroscopy. Ni-MOF exhibits four different peaks at 860 cm−1, 1137 cm−1, 1430 cm−1, 1612 cm−1, corresponding to C–H, C–C, –COO, and organic benzene rings [31–33]. This further indicates that Ni-MOF has been successfully prepared [34]. And 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 has the same peak as Ni-MOF at these four positions, indicating that Ni-MOF has successfully synthesized into 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the FT-IR plots of CQDs with absorption peaks at the vicinity of 3440, 1615, 1400, and 1040 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of –NH2/-OH, CC/CO, C–N, and C–O, respectively [35]. Absorption peaks at the neighborhood of 2970, 2910 cm−1 correspond to the C–H stretching vibration. The absorption peaks at the vicinity of 1080, 535, and 472 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of SO, S–S, and –SH, respectively [36–38]. It can be seen that CQDs have abundant hydrophilic functional groups on the surface, indicating that CQDs have excellent water solubility, and the prepared S-CQDs have SO, S–S, and –SH functional groups on the surface, which indicates that in situ doping of sulfur has been successfully. The intensity of the C–N and –NH2 stretching vibrations of the NS-CQDs is significantly stronger than that of the S-CQDs. –NH2 stretching vibration strengths of NS-CQDs, indicating that NS-CQDs nitrogen has been successfully doped.

Fig. 3(b) shows the absorption peaks at the vicinity of 2970, 1506, 825, and 745 cm−1, which corresponds to the C–H, CC, –OH, and C–H stretching vibrations, respectively [39]. The absorption peaks near 1580, 1390 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of –COOH. The surface of the samples is rich in hydrophilic functional groups, which will facilitate the full dissolution of the samples in aqueous solution and the smooth progress of the photocatalytic hydrogen precipitation reaction. The stretching vibrations of Zr–O and Ni–O near 662 and 554 cm−1 [40,41] indicate that Co/Zr-MOF and Ni-MOF have been successfully prepared. 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 has the stretching vibrations of the absorption peaks of Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF at the same time, which indicates that the 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 has been successfully prepared.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the basic morphology of Ni-MOF, the single Ni-MOF presents a nanoflower-like structure with a large number of folds on the surface, which can provide more attachment space for the co-catalysts and can provide massive reactive active sites. Co/Zr-MOF in Fig. 4 (b), which presents a three-dimensional polyhedral structure with well-defined particles and smooth surface. The average particle size of Co/Zr-MOF was counted by using particle size analysis software, and it was found that the average diameter of Co/Zr-MOF was about 48.35 nm. Fig. 4 (c) shows the SEM of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2, the composite catalyst 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 possesses the morphology that both Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF have. Fig. 4 (d) shows a locally enlarged SEM image of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2, where attached Co/Zr-MOF nanopolyhedra can be seen on Ni-MOF nanoflowers and Co/Zr-MOF can be distributed between the folds of multiple nanoflowers, which are successfully composite with Ni-MOF. The NS-CQDs nanoparticles in Fig. 4 (e) have a complete and uniformly distributed structure. Fig. 4 (f) and (g) TEM images of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2, it was found that polyhedral particles Co/Zr-MOF can be uniformly distributed on Ni-MOF nanoflower sheets, and Ni-MOF nanoflowers are clear and complete. After Co/Zr-MOF modification, the shape of Ni-MOF nanoflowers did not change. After modification, the Ni-MOF nanosheet structure remains intact. The Co/Zr-MOF particles on Ni-MOF nanoflowers can provide many catalytic reaction sites, which facilitate charge transfer and improve the performance of catalytic reactions [42]. Fig. 4 shows the elemental mapping of (h) and (i), and in Fig. (i) the elemental mapping contains Zr, Co, Ni, C, N, and S, and Zr, Co, Ni, C, N, and S are uniformly distributed. Among them, the NS-CQDs mainly contained the elements C, N, and S. The C, N, and S in the elemental mapping diagram of Fig. 4(i) can fully indicate that the NS-CQDs were successfully loaded on top of the NCZ2, which further illustrates the successful preparation of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2.

Fig. 5 shows the results of BET analysis of each sample, Fig. 5 (a) shows the nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves, it can be seen that the samples appeared hysteresis loops during the nitrogen adsorption and desorption process, and the type of the curve of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 is type IV isothermal, and the appearance of the H3 type of hysteresis loops, which can indicate that the 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 has rich mesoporous structure [43]. Fig. 5(b) shows the pore size distribution curves of the samples, and the results of the pore size distribution of the curves indicate that the prepared catalysts are mesoporous structures, which can provide a large number of available active sites for the reaction and effective mass transfer [44,45]. In Table 2 the specific surface area of Co/Zr-MOF was the largest, as high as 695.36 m2 g−1, the Ni-MOF was smaller at 91.77 m2 g−1, and the NCZ2 was 456.06 m2 g−1, and the Co/Zr-MOF provided a larger specific surface area for the composite catalysts. Due to the significant advantage of Co/Zr-MOF in specific surface area. From the SEM test results in the article, it can be found that Co/Zr-MOF grows on the surface of Ni-MOF in the composite catalyst. Therefore, after modification with Co/Zr-MOF, the specific surface area of NCZ2 was significantly increased compared to Ni-MOF. The specific surface area of NCZ2 is significantly larger than that of Ni-MOF, which can provide more active sites for the reaction and promote photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 was 461.76 m2 g−1, which appeared to be elevated compared with that of NCZ2 due to the incorporation of NS-CQDs, which adhered to the surface of NCZ2. And the incorporation of NS-CQDs also enhanced the pore volume and average pore diameter of NCZ2. NS-CQDs can provide more active sites for the hydrogen production reaction and accelerate the reaction rate.

In order to analyze the elemental composition of the CQDs and each catalyst, as well as the surface chemical state between each catalyst, XPS tests were performed. Fig. 6 (a) shows the CQDs were mainly composed of C, O, N, and S elements. The 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 has both characteristic peaks exhibited by Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF, indicating that 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 is mainly composed of Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF.

Fig. 6(b) show the N 1s spectrum of CQDs, with three peaks, namely pyridine nitrogen (394.89–397.24 eV), pyrrole nitrogen (398.33–399.79 eV), and graphite nitrogen (400.90–402.21 eV) [46,47]. The presence of pyridine nitrogen can increase the structural defects, pyrrole nitrogen will provide electrons, and graphite nitrogen will reduce the band gap, and the doping of these three forms of nitrogen can effectively improve the catalytic performance of CQDs. From Table 3 , by comparing the changes in the relative content of nitrogen forms of CQDs before and after doping, it is found that among the three forms of nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen has the largest proportion. After elemental doping, the proportion of pyrrole nitrogen increased significantly, which can indicate that the doped nitrogen is mainly added in the form of pyrrole nitrogen. 53.89% of pyrrole nitrogen in S-CQDs and 67.21% of pyrrole nitrogen in NS-CQDs, and the proportion of pyrrole nitrogen was increased by 13.32% after doping. Pyrrole nitrogen is abundant in NS-CQDs, which can be more convenient to carry out the electron transfer NS-CQDs are rich in pyrrole nitrogen, which can facilitate electron transfer, and the pyrrole nitrogen also has the potential to catalyze proton-coupled electron transfer reactions.

Fig. 6(c) shows the S 2p spectrum of the CQDs, which are sulfate sulfur (168.5–169.5) eV, sulfone (167.5–168.5) eV, sulfoxide (165.0–166.0) eV, thiophene (164.0–164.4) eV, and pyrite sulfur (163.6–164.0) eV [48]. As shown in Table 3, the percentage of organic sulfur (sulfone, sulfoxide, thiophene) in NS-CQDs is 66.58%. In situ doping of sulfur-containing coal-based CQDs can be achieved, and the introduction of sulfur atoms with larger diameters will lead to deformation of the carbon skeleton, resulting in an abundance of thiophene defects, which would be more conducive to hydrogen-producing reactions. The C 1s spectrum for CQDs will not be detailed separately here, as it has been explained in detail in the article [49].

The Co 2p spectra of the catalysts are shown in Fig. 6(d), from which the Co 2p spectra of Co/Zr-MOF at 781.50 eV and 796.80 eV, corresponding to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, and satellite peaks at 787.00 eV and 803.27 eV are shown [50]. The Co 2p spectra of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 at 781.60 eV and 796.90 eV, corresponding to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, with satellite peaks at 787.10 eV and 803.37 eV. The Zr 3 d spectra was shown in Fig. 6(e), from which the Zr 3 d spectra of Co/Zr-MOF at 185.28 eV and 182.9 eV for Zr 3d3/2 and Zr 3d5/2 [51]. The Zr 3 d spectra of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 at 185.38 eV and 183.05 eV for Zr 3d3/2 and Zr 3d5/2. The Co 2p and Zr 3 d characteristic peaks in 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 were shifted towards higher binding energies compared to Co/Zr-MOF.

The Ni 2p spectra of the catalysts are shown in Fig. 6(f), from Fig. 6(f), the Ni 2p spectra of Ni-MOF at 856.30 eV and 873.97 eV corresponded to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, and 861.73 eV and 880.10 eV were the satellite peaks [52]. The Ni 2p spectra of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 at 856.20 eV and 873.77 eV corresponds to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, and 861.63 eV and 880.00 eV are satellite peaks. The Ni 2p characteristic peaks in 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 are shifted towards lower binding energy compared to Ni-MOF.

In summary, the Co 2p and Zr 3 d characteristic peaks in 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 are shifted towards higher binding energies compared to Co/Zr-MOF. Ni 2p characteristic peak in 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 is shifted towards lower binding energy compared to Ni-MOF. A change in the electron binding energy occurs, indicating that the interaction between Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF occurs, attributed to the electron transfer from Co/Zr-MOF to Ni-MOF, and the density of the outer electron cloud of Ni, Co, and Zr atoms in Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF will be changed. CQDs are good electron transfer media, and the addition of CQDs will greatly promote the electron transport. The addition of CQDs, which is a good electron transfer medium, will greatly promote the electron transfer. A built-in electric field will be formed between NS-CQDs, Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF, which will promote the transfer of photogenerated electrons under the excitation of light. This results in a strong coupling interaction between NS-CQDs, Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF, forming a strongly coupled interface [53]. It can be hypothesized that a heterostructure is formed between NS-CQDs, Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF.

Fig. 7 shows the test results of photocatalytic hydrogen precipitation experiment. From Fig. 7(a), the hydrogen precipitation of composite catalysts showed different degrees of improvement compared with single catalysts Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF, and the optimal composite catalyst was NCZ2. In order to further optimize the catalysts, the composite catalysts are further based on the composite catalysts as NCZ2, and further composite CQDs. The test results are shown in Fig. 7(b), where different kinds of CQDs will be introduced to modify NCZ2, and it was found that the photocatalytic decomposition of water precipitation of hydrogen appeared to be significantly enhanced after NCZ2 was modified by NS-CQDs. That is, the amount of hydrogen precipitated by the 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 reached 102.51 μmol at the fifth hour. At the fifth hour, the amount of hydrogen precipitation of Ni-MOF was only 19.17 μmol, and that of Co/Zr-MOF reached 28.39 μmol. In terms of hydrogen generation from the catalyst, 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 at the fifth hour is 5.35 times higher than that of single Ni-MOF and 3.61 times higher than that of single Co/Zr-MOF. In Fig. 7(c), it is found that the optimal amount of CQDs added is 5%. The reason is that the addition of too many CQDs will cause the active sites on the surface of NCZ2 to be covered, resulting in a buildup on the surface of NCZ2, which in turn affects the hydrogen production activity. In Fig. 7 (d), the optimal pH for 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 is 9, with a rate of 3662.68 μmol g−1 h−1. In order to test the stability of the catalysts in the hydrogen precipitation, Fig. 7(e) shows the results of hydrogen cycling experiments of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 after four hydrogen precipitation cycles, and it can be seen that 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 still has a stable activity at 20 h. The catalysts before and after the reaction were subjected to XRD tests, and the results are shown in Fig. 7(f) XRD plots of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 before and after the reaction. It was found that the catalyst after the reaction did not disappear the obvious characteristic peaks and no new peaks appeared after the reaction, which is sufficient to show that the prepared catalyst has good structural stability.

In Fig. 8 (a), the fluorescence intensity from high to low is as follows: EY, Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF, NCZ2, and 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2. The fluorescence intensity of NCZ2 was greater than 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2, indicating that the modification of NCZ2 by NS-CQDs can effectively slow down the electron-hole complexation for the hydrogen production reaction process. The highest carrier separation efficiency was achieved with 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 due to the addition of NS-CQDs adds new active sites, provides a bridge for efficient electron transport, reduces the distance of electron transport, induces charge transfer, and accelerates the generation of electron-hole pairs [54]. And the heterostructure may be formed between NS-CQDs, Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF, which facilitates the charge transfer [55].

Fig. 8 (b) shows the TRPL, and the sample parameters in Table 4 were obtained using the method described in the literature [56]. The shorter average lifetime implies faster decay kinetics. This performance enhancement can be attributed to the introduction of NS-CQDs, which act as both bridges to transport electrons during the electron transport process. NS-CQDs can accelerate charge separation and increase the number of electrons. The average fluorescence lifetimes, in descending order, were EY, Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF, NCZ2, and 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2. The comparison shows that the NS-CQDs can shorten the fluorescence lifetime by the addition of NS-CQDs, which increases the surface active sites and shortens the distance of interfacial electron transfer.

Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF, NS-CQDs, NCZ2, and 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2, electrochemical performance tests were performed. The photoelectrochemical test results are shown in Fig. 9 , and Fig. 9 (a) shows the transient photocurrent response diagram, which was carried out to investigate the charge transfer efficiency of the samples. It was found that different photocurrent responses were presented during five irradiation cycles with the light on and off. The photocurrent responses were 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2, NCZ2, NS-CQDs, Co/Zr-MOF, and Ni-MOF in descending order. The composite catalyst 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 showed the highest photocurrent and the highest charge transfer efficiency [57], which shows that the incorporation of NS-CQDs facilitates the photogenerated carrier separation. Fig. 9(b) shows the EIS test results, and the impedance radii are 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2, NCZ2, NS-CQDs, Co/Zr-MOF, and Ni-MOF in descending order. It can be seen that the impedance radius of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 is the smallest, with optimal optoelectronic ability, and the smallest resistance for the charge transfer, which accelerates the reduction of the electrons to H+ [58]. Fig. 9(c) shows the LSV test results of Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF, NCZ2, and 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2, and 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 was found to have the lowest overpotential, which can indicate that 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 is more advantageous in photocatalytic hydrogen precipitation [59]. 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 has a lower overpotential due to the fact that the addition of NS-CQDs is favorable to improve the charge transfer ability and the possible formation of heterojunction between Co/Zr-MOF and Ni-MOF, which accelerates the charge transfer rate between the catalyst interfaces.

Fig. 9 (d) shows the results of zeta potential test of CQDs, and it is found that the zeta values of all the tested CQDs are negative, which indicates that the surface of CQDs is negatively charged. The reason for this is that the surface of CQDs prepared from coal carbon as a carbon source is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, which results in the surface charge of CQDs exhibiting negative values [60]. The larger the absolute value of zeta of CQDs, the more stable it is in the system, and the more favorable it is to carry out the stable catalytic reaction. The absolute value of zeta of NS-CQDs is the largest, which indicates that NS-CQDs are highly stable in the system.

Fig. 10 (a), (b) and (c) show the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (inset shows the bandgap diagram) by using the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra to analyze the energy bandgap of the catalysts. According to the Kubelka Munk function [61], the bandgap diagrams were obtained, and the bandgaps of Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF, and NS-CQDs were approximately 3.20 eV, 3.52 eV, and 2.45 eV, respectively.

Mott-Schottky plots of Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF and NS-CQDs are shown in Fig. 10(d), (e) and (f). According to the positive tangent slope of the Mott-Schottky curves of Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF, and NS-CQDs, Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF, and NS-CQDs are n-type semiconductors [62]. From the tangent line, the Efb of Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF and NS-CQDs are −1.27 eV, −0.70 eV and −0.60 eV, respectively. The ECB can be obtained as −1.47 eV, −0.90 eV, and −0.80 eV (with respect to saturated calomel electrode (SCE)) for Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF, and NS-CQDs, respectively. Because ENHE = ESCE + 0.24 eV, i.e., ECB is −1.23 eV, −0.66 eV, and −0.56 eV, respectively (with respect to the standard hydrogen electrode (NHE)).

From the results of the above analysis, the energy band gap structures of NS-CQDs, Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF can be known, and the energy band structures of the three are interleaved with each other, as shown in the diagram of the photocatalytic hydrogen precipitation mechanism of the 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 system in Fig. 11 . After NS-CQDs, Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF are in contact with each other, due to the existence of Fermi energy level difference, electron transfer will occur between NS-CQDs and Ni-MOF, and between Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF, until the Fermi energy levels between them reach equilibrium [63]. NS-CQDs and Co/Zr-MOF will gain electrons and Ni-MOF will lose electrons, resulting in a built-in electric field at the interfaces between NS-CQDs and Ni-MOF, and Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF. The existence of built-in electric fields provides a charge transfer path for electron transfer, which can effectively reduce the resistance of charge transfer and promote the transfer of photo generated electrons [64]. The formation of built-in electric fields further provides a driving force for electron transfer, enabling convenient and efficient electron transfer [65]. NS-CQDs and Co/Zr-MOF will show downward bending of energy bands after getting electrons, and similarly Ni-MOF will show upward bending of energy bands after losing electrons, and it can be hypothesized that a double S-type heterojunction is formed between NS-CQDs, Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF [66]. From this, the photocatalytic hydrogen precipitation mechanism of 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 can be deduced, and the semiconductor will generate electron-hole pairs under the excitation of visible light, and the photogenerated electrons will be transferred from VB toward CB and complexed with holes. The excess available electrons at the CB of NS-CQDs and Co/Zr-MOF will be transferred to the VB of Ni-MOF through the built-in electric field and then to the CB, where hydrogen will be generated at the CB of Ni-MOF. The EY molecules adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst are first excited by light to form a singlet excited state EY1*, and then transform into a more stable triplet excited state EY3*. Under the action of TEOA, EY3* quenches into EY−· with strong reducing ability. After providing electrons to the system, EY−· returns to the ground state EY [67,68]. The EY−
generated electrons will be transferred to the CBs of NS-CQDs, Ni-MOF and Co/Zr-MOF, which will provide more available electrons for the internal electron transfer within the catalysts, thus ensuring that efficient decomposition of water for hydrogen production will be carried out.

In this study, NS-CQDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method with high-sulfur lignite as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source. The NS-CQDs were used to modify Ni-MOF with cobalt ion-doped Zr-MOF (Co/Zr-MOF), and the composite photocatalysts were used for photocatalytic decomposition of hydrogen precipitation from water. It was found that the highest hydrogen precipitation rate of the 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 reached 3662.68 μmol g−1 h−1. In terms of hydrogen generation from the catalyst, 5%NS-CQDs/NCZ2 at the fifth hour is 5.35 times higher than that of single Ni-MOF and 3.61 times higher than that of single Co/Zr-MOF. From the positions of the conduction and valence bands between Ni-MOF, Co/Zr-MOF and NS-CQDs, it can be hypothesized that a double S-type heterojunction was formed among the three. In situ sulfur doping of carbon quantum dots can generate abundant thiophene defects, the N-element doped CQDs with abundant pyrrole nitrogen are more convenient to carry out the electron transfer and catalyze the proton-coupled electron transfer reaction. Thereby, NS-CQDs are more favorable for improving electron transport and reducing the compounding rate of electron-hole pairs in photocatalytic hydrogen precipitation.
